title: What Is a Super Blood Wolf Moon
subtitle: 超级血狼月到底是什么？天文奇观还是标题党

paragraphs.en: Blue moons, rose moons, supermoons. For some reason all the websites seem to think you really want to know all about these moons. "Catch This Weekend's AMAZING SUPERMOON," one headline will announce. "The Supermoon Isn't Actually A Big Deal And You're All Ruining Astronomy," another will complain.
paragraphs.zh: 蓝月亮、玫瑰月亮、超级月亮。不知为何，所有网站似乎都觉得你特别想了解这些月亮的种种。“来看本周末惊艳的超级月亮！”一个标题如此宣布。另一个则抱怨道：“超级月亮根本没什么大不了，你们这是在毁掉天文学。”

paragraphs.en: The latest example is the super blood wolf moon eclipse which sounds like the name of an emotional song I would have performed a modern dance routine to in 9th grade, but is, in fact, an astronomical event set to occur on January 20. As we'll explain below, there is nothing truly mysterious or otherwise special about this moon, and it will not look like a bloody wolf head in the sky or anything wild like that.
paragraphs.zh: 最新的例子是“超级血狼月食”，这名字听起来像是我九年级时会配着跳现代舞的一首情感歌曲，而实际上，它是定于1月20日发生的一次天文事件。正如下文将解释的，这个月亮本身并无真正的神秘或特别之处，它不会像血淋淋的狼头一样悬挂天空，也不会是任何类似的狂野景象。

paragraphs.en: We get these super lunar events by smashing together all the qualifiers we've historically used to keep track of full moons throughout the year, and in the age of the internet we can get a little too enthusiastic. Consider this your go-to resource for all moon-gazing news. Here's what you need to know about the latest lunar event.
paragraphs.zh: 这些“超级”月相事件，不过是我们把历史上用来标记全年满月的各种修饰词一股脑堆砌在一起的结果，而在互联网时代，人们未免有些过于狂热了。就把本文当作你赏月资讯的首选参考吧。以下是你需要了解的关于这次最新月相事件的知识。

paragraphs.en: Look, it's okay if you don't know. There are probably loads of folks who walk around pretending they totally know why that thing in the sky seems to get bigger and smaller at regular intervals but who totally do not. The moon orbits Earth, and it's tidally locked — that means it always shows us the same face, instead of spinning around like our planet does.
paragraphs.zh: 听着，不知道也没关系。大街上恐怕多的是人假装完全明白天上那东西为啥会周期性地变大变小，实则一无所知。月球围绕地球运行，并且处于潮汐锁定状态——这意味着它总是以同一面朝向地球，而不像我们这颗行星那样自转。

paragraphs.en: That's why you can always see the man on the moon (or the moon rabbit, depending on your cultural preferences) even as it spins around us. But while the moon is big and bright in the sky when it's full, that's only because it's reflecting light from the sun. But the moon is always moving, so it's getting hit with sunlight at different angles.
paragraphs.zh: 正因如此，即使月球围绕我们旋转，你总能看见月亮上的人脸（或玉兔，视你的文化偏好而定）。不过，满月时月亮在空中又大又亮，那仅仅是因为它反射了太阳光。但月球一直在运动，所以它接收阳光的角度也在不断变化。

paragraphs.en: It's invisible to us during the new moon, because our satellite is parked right between us and the sun; the so-called dark side of the moon is lit up like Las Vegas, but the side we can see is in shadow. A full moon happens when the earth is right between the sun and the moon, so sunlight hits the part we can see. And all the other phases are just the transition from one of those extremes to the other.
paragraphs.zh: 新月时我们看不见它，因为我们的这颗卫星恰好停在地球和太阳之间；所谓月球的暗面此时灯火通明宛如拉斯维加斯，但我们能看到的那一面却处于阴影之中。当地球正好位于太阳和月亮之间时，满月便发生了，阳光恰好照射到我们能看到的部分。而其他所有月相，不过是这两种极端状态之间的过渡而已。

paragraphs.en: The moon isn't always exactly the same distance from Earth, because its orbit isn't perfectly circular. We call the closest point perigee, and the most distant point is apogee. 2018's closest perigee and most distant apogee both happened in January, and the difference was about 30,000 miles.
paragraphs.zh: 月球与地球的距离并非始终一致，因为它的轨道并非完美的圆形。距离最近的点我们称为近地点，最远的点则是远地点。2018年最近的近地点和最远的远地点都出现在一月份，两者相差约3万英里。

paragraphs.en: The reason you care about this ordinary change in distance is that it turns a moon super. When a full moon happens close to perigee, it's going to look a bit bigger. Honestly, the difference is not that profound, but if you're in a position to photograph the supermoon next to something that shows the slight increase in scale, it can look pretty cool.
paragraphs.zh: 你之所以在意这种寻常的距离变化，是因为它让月亮变成了“超级月亮”。当满月发生在近地点附近时，它看起来会略大一些。老实说，差别并没有那么显著，但如果你有机会把超级月亮和某个能衬托出其尺寸略有增加的物体拍在一起，效果还是相当酷的。

paragraphs.en: January's super blood wolf moon eclipse is super because the date lines up with the closest the moon will get to us during January, but the moon won't actually be at its closest for the year until February's supermoon, which you can expect plenty of undue excitement over. Blood moons only occur during total lunar eclipses.
paragraphs.zh: 一月的“超级血狼月食”之所以“超级”，是因为日期与一月里月亮最接近地球的时刻重合，但月亮真正达到全年最近距离，还要等到二月的超级月亮——届时你肯定又会看到一大波小题大做的兴奋。血月只发生在月全食期间。

paragraphs.en: When the moon slips through our shadow, we give it a reddish coloring. The moon can also look orange whenever it's rising or setting, or if it hangs low in the horizon all night — the light bouncing off of it has to travel through a thicker atmosphere there, which scatters more blue light away. But you'll probably only see that deep, sinister red during an eclipse.
paragraphs.zh: 当月亮滑入地球的阴影中时，我们便看到它染上了一层微红色。月亮在升起或落下时，或者整夜低垂在地平线附近时，也会呈现橙色——从月面反射的光必须穿过那里更厚的大气层，从而散射掉更多的蓝光。但你大概只有在月食期间，才能看到那种深沉、诡异的猩红色。

paragraphs.en: A lot of headlines about moons are just ridiculous, but you should definitely roll out of bed to look at a blood moon if one is going to be visible in your region, even though they're just lunar eclipses and not evidence of bloody battles between the sky gods. In March of 2018, we had our second "blue moon" of the year, to much acclaim.
paragraphs.zh: 很多关于月亮的头条标题纯属荒谬可笑，但如果血月在你所在地区可见，那你绝对值得从床上爬起来看一眼，尽管它们只是月食，而非天神之间血腥争斗的证据。2018年3月，我们迎来了当年第二个“蓝月亮”，备受赞誉。

paragraphs.en: A blue moon is a nickname for when two full moons fall in the same calendar month, and we hadn't previously had two in one year since 1999. We won't have it happen again until 2037. Astronomer David Chapman explained that this is merely a peculiarity of our calendar.
paragraphs.zh: “蓝月”是同一个日历月内出现两次满月的昵称，而上一次一年之内出现两次蓝月还是在1999年。下一次要等到2037年才会再次发生。天文学家戴维·查普曼解释说，这仅仅是我们历法的一个奇特之处。

paragraphs.en: Once we stopped doing things based on the moon and started trying to follow the sun and the seasons, we stopped having one reliable full moon per month. The moon cycle is 29.53 days long on average, so in most months we still end up with a single new moon and a single full one. But every once in a while, one month steals a full moon from another.
paragraphs.zh: 一旦人类不再根据月亮行事，转而尝试追随太阳和季节，我们就不再每月拥有一次可靠的满月了。月相周期平均为29.53天，因此在大多数月份里，我们依然能看到一次新月和一次满月。但每隔一段时间，某个月份就会从另一个月份那里“偷走”一次满月。

paragraphs.en: In 2018 both January and March stacked full moons on the first and last nights of the month, leaving February in the dark. Getting two blue moons a year is rare, but we have individual blue moons every few years. Also, fun fact: not actually blue.
paragraphs.zh: 2018年，一月和三月都在月初和月末出现了满月，而二月则没有满月。一年出现两次蓝月相当罕见，但单独的蓝月每隔几年就会出现一次。另外，说个有趣的事实：蓝月并非真的是蓝色。

paragraphs.en: A moon can indeed take on a moody blue color, but this only happens when particles of just the right size disperse through the sky. Big clouds of ash from volcanic eruptions or fires can do the trick, but it doesn't happen often. You may have heard that the super special second blue moon of 2018 was also a Paschal moon.
paragraphs.zh: 月亮确实可能呈现出一种阴郁的蓝色，但这只有当大气中弥漫着大小恰到好处的颗粒物时才会发生。火山喷发或大火产生的大片灰云可以造成这种效果，但情况并不常见。你可能听说过，2018年那个超级特别的第二个蓝月同时也是“逾越节月亮”。

paragraphs.en: That just means it was the first full moon of spring, which is often used to determine the date of Easter Sunday. All of this is just calendar nonsense and we refuse to go into it further. Sometimes you'll see a headline that promises a moon with so many qualifiers it makes your head spin.
paragraphs.zh: 这仅仅意味着它是春天的第一个满月，常被用来确定复活节的日期。这一切无非是历法上的文字游戏，我们拒绝进一步深究。有时你会看到某些标题，承诺的月亮带着一大堆修饰词，多到让你头晕目眩。

paragraphs.en: Lots of websites will tell you that "wolf moon" is the traditional name of the first full moon of the year in "Native American" cultures, which is kind of a weird thing to claim. The idea that hungry, howling wolves were such a universal constant in January that all of North America spontaneously came up with the same nickname is illogical.
paragraphs.zh: 很多网站会告诉你，“狼月”是“美洲原住民”文化中对一年中第一个满月的传统称呼——这种说法其实挺奇怪的。认为饥饿嚎叫的狼群在一月是如此普遍的存在，以至于整个北美各地拥有不同文化、地理和语言的族群都不约而同地想出了同一个绰号，这完全不合逻辑。

paragraphs.en: Many cultures have traditional names for the full moon in a given month or season, so there's quite a list to draw from if you're trying to really juice up a story on a slightly-bigger-than-average view of the moon. But these are all based on human calendars and activities and folklore; you will not go outside and see a pink moon in April, though I wish it were so.
paragraphs.zh: 许多文化确实对特定月份或季节的满月有传统称呼，所以如果你想为一个只比平时稍大一点的月亮写篇添油加醋的报道，确实有不少素材可挖。但这一切都基于人类的历法、活动和民间传说；你并不会在四月走出家门就看到一轮粉红色的月亮，尽管我也希望如此。

words.meaning: phr. 使更有趣；添油加醋
